schools.summary: The scene follows directly on the flight from the temple (John 8:59) on the sabbath; Robertson dates it before the feast of dedication and notes the only NT case of congenital blindness healed (a Messianic sign, Mt 11:5).
Chrysostom: Christ, rejected in the temple, goes out and heals the blind man so His absence may cool their fury and the miracle soften them; He seeks the beggar out.
Calvin: the Evangelist mingles doctrine with the miracle to show its fruit; congenital, humanly incurable blindness magnifies Christ's power.
Augustine: the narrative is so rich that a day would not suffice to expound it.
Henry: Christ takes notice of the piteous case of one who could not see Him.
Lightfoot/Barnes give the rabbinic axiom that calamity is the wage of sin (Luke 13:1-4); Robertson notes the disciples are merely puzzled, not expecting a cure.
Clarke and Wesley find behind the question the Pharisaic notion of transmigration / a pre-existent sin, held 'without any ground from Scripture.'
Calvin: though all suffering proceeds from sin, we err in three ways — bitterly censuring, condemning all the afflicted, and missing that God may chasten the guiltless.

debates.question: What assumption lies behind 'who did sin?'

sides.position: the rabbinic link of calamity to a definite sin
a pre-existent sin / transmigration of souls

schools.summary: The denial is bounded by the question — neither's special sin caused the blindness; Robertson: disease is sometimes from sin (John 5:14) but this case is blame-free, and there is comfort in it.
Calvin: Christ does not call them sinless but forbids seeking the cause in sin; God may have another object than punishment, so restrain curiosity when causes are hidden.
Clarke: the blindness becomes the instrument of his salvation and God's glory; Wesley: not for his or his parents' sins, but that God's power be displayed.
Robertson and Ellicott prefer 'we must work' — Christ associating the disciples with Himself in the Father's task; the 'day' is the appointed season, 'night' its close.
Calvin: the figure is drawn from daily labour (Ps 104:22-23); the 'day' is the time the Father fixed for Christ's working.
Wesley: night = after this life, when no man can work out salvation, though Christ can work always; Clarke dates it about six months before the cross.

debates.question: Is the reading 'I must work' or 'we must work'?

sides.position: 'we must work' — Christ joining the disciples to His mission

schools.summary: 'When I am in the world' — the true Light cannot be present without shining (John 1:5); Robertson distinguishes the Incarnation here from the Son as ever the Light (John 1:4); Barnes' sublime sun-image.
Clarke/Wesley keep it practical: like the sun, Christ dispenses light everywhere, teaching by Spirit, preaching, and example (John 8:12).
Calvin: spoken by anticipation — though His working time is bounded, He is the sun illuminating the whole earth; the coming cure is the sign of that light.
The Jews held saliva medicinal for the eyes but forbade medicine on the sabbath, deepening the offense; the clay had no natural power over congenital blindness, so it is sign, not surgery.
Calvin: the seemingly absurd means 'doubles' the blindness to try the man's faith and obedience, who obeys on a bare word.
Wesley: the clay could sooner blind than cure, teaching that God is no farther from the event whether He works with means or without.
Lightfoot/Barnes on Siloam = 'Sent' (Neh 3:15); the waters had no efficacy, the sending tested obedience; Robertson denies the cure lay in clay or washing.
Calvin: no power in clay or pool, but Christ uses outward symbols to accustom believers to signs and to show all things at His disposal; 'Sent' points to Christ the Sent One.
Wesley: the errand across the city made the miracle widely noticed; the man's ready obedience to a bare word is the point.
The better reading is 'beggar' for 'blind'; the man was widely known, so the cure could be no imposture; Lightfoot adds the beggar's customary cry.
Chrysostom: God's condescension in healing the poorest and meanest, stopping the Jews' mouths, for He came for the salvation of all.
Calvin: the well-known beggar spread the miracle's fame, yet impiety obscures God's works, so some denied his identity.
Henry: the street-debate is providential confirmation — what was first doubted is later admitted without scruple.
Ellicott prefers 'No, but he is like him'; Robertson hears a vigorous denial and a divided crowd, with the emphatic 'that one spake up' (vv.11,25,36); the man's 'I am he' settles it.
Clarke: the miracle was public and not lightly credited; the division was only over the man's identity, not the fact of blindness or of present sight.

A natural, logical question: the fact conceded, only the 'how' is sought; the question presupposes they knew him as the blind beggar (v.8), and begins the pattern repeated by the Pharisees (vv.15,26).

themes: the work stands as plain fact

schools.summary: The man knows only 'the man called Jesus,' not yet the Messiah (v.36); 'received sight' literally 'see again'; his artless account omits what he could not have watched.
Calvin: the happy result of obedience urges us to surmount every obstacle and proceed courageously where the Lord calls.
Wesley: he was before totally ignorant of Jesus — the small starting-point of a growing confession.
Robertson: the same hostile question as John 7:11; Ellicott: the man's honest ignorance, since Jesus had not returned after sending him to the pool (v.7).
Gill: the question aimed to seize Christ for sabbath-work; He had withdrawn to avoid both their rage and popular applause.
Clarke: they designed to deliver Jesus to the Sanhedrin as a law-breaker.

themes: the man left to witness alone

schools.summary: The Pharisees here = the Sanhedrin (Lightfoot); the professional teachers (Robertson); the man was brought to accuse Jesus of sabbath-breaking, in a spirit of opposition, not to weigh the evidence.
Henry: the miracle that should have settled Christ's reputation instead has Him prosecuted as a criminal.
Calvin: wicked men do not profit from God's works but pour out venom; the cure that should soften hearts of stone only hardens them.
For the rabbis the sabbath fact outweighed the cure; making clay and anointing with saliva were both forbidden on the day under the minute sabbath rules; Ellicott ties it to the great feast-day.
Calvin: Christ purposely chose the sabbath (as in John 5), since the malicious defense would magnify God's power — the offense a whetstone sharpening their inquiry.
Wesley/Clarke: anointing the eyes with medicine on the sabbath was particularly forbidden by the tradition of the elders.
The right question was whether, not how (as with conversion); the man answers briefly, against his will, now consciously possessing sight; only interest in the 'how,' no denial of the fact.
Calvin: the Pharisees drop the question of fact and dispute only the law, finding a crime in the sabbath circumstance.

themes: disputing the law not the fact

schools.summary: One party: sabbath-breaker, so 'not of God' (John 5:10,16); the other repeats Nicodemus (John 3:2); Barnes/Clarke expose the false premise — they assumed their sabbath interpretation infallible, though the day was made for man.
Calvin: 'sinner' means an immoral despiser of God (Mark 2:16); Wesley: one living in wilful sin. The miracle divides the very tribunal assembled to condemn.

debates.question: Could Jesus be 'of God' given the sabbath-breach?

sides.position: no — a sabbath-breaker is not from God
a sinner could not do such signs

schools.summary: The Pharisees quietly shift from the sabbath to 'who opened your eyes'; the man reasons that such a work proves Christ sent from God; the doctors disagree, so they ask the patient.
Chrysostom notes they abandon the sabbath charge they cannot hold and ask instead about the opening of his eyes.
Calvin: the more they inquire, the brighter truth burns — like trying to blow out a strong flame; the man's confession advances to 'a prophet.'
The cure conflicted with their theology, so 'the Jews' (the term of opposition) disbelieve the fact itself and summon the parents with three intimidating questions.
The Catena: truth is strengthened by the snares laid against it, while a lie betrays itself.
Calvin: their blindness was voluntary, nothing hindering them but perverse hatred of Christ; Henry: the fear of man is a snare (Prov 29:25).
A calculated triple question (your son? born blind? how then see?) with emphatic 'ye,' meant to confuse the parents and give the Pharisees a handle.
Calvin: the Lord defeats the craft and turns it to the opposite purpose, the parents answering only the half they safely can.
The parents clearly affirm sonship and congenital blindness, cutting the ground from the Pharisees' disbelief; the third they cannot answer, not being eyewitnesses and afraid.
Calvin: they pass over the cure to avoid offense, and by their silence show ingratitude, burying God's grace through terror.
They profess ignorance of the 'how' and 'who,' answering in the question's exact words, refusing to be entangled in the real point; 'of age' = full-grown, reckoned at thirty.
Calvin: fear of excommunication drives them to push the witness onto their son — 'ask him' true yet cowardly, throwing the testimony back on the bold beggar.
A continuing fear (John 7:13) and a settled compact of the leaders (not a formal decree); the penalty was the lesser excommunication (nidui), the anathema (cherem) coming only after the resurrection.
Calvin: excommunication is an ancient, legitimate discipline against apostates, here wickedly turned against Christ's disciples.
Wesley: to be put out of the synagogue is to be excommunicated — the fear silencing the parents.
'For this reason' — fear of the synagogue-ban is caution enough; the parents cling to the safe formula 'ask him,' setting the son alone before the council.

themes: parents' caution vs the son's coming boldness

schools.summary: 'Give God the praise' is an adjuration to tell the truth (Josh 7:19; 1 Sam 6:5), not thanks (unlike Luke 17:18); a demand pointless unless implying his story was false.
The Catena: under a show of religion they want him to confess Christ had nothing to do with the work — which Augustine calls blasphemy, not glory to God.
Calvin: shame constrains them to recall the firm man; the more they struggle against God, the more cords they put about their own neck.
Henry: lasting love for Christ arises from actual knowledge — comfort in 'whereas I was blind, now I see.'

themes: whereas I was blind now I see

schools.summary: The keen man refuses the trap, passing by the 'sinner' quibble and clinging to the one fact of his experience, confining himself to certain knowledge they cannot gainsay.
Calvin: he speaks ironically to wound them, suspending judgment on the person and letting the undeniable fact argue for itself.

debates.question: What is the tone of 'whether he be a sinner I know not'?

schools.summary: Repeated questioning aimed to draw him into contradiction or intimidate him, but grace gave him strength to confound his examiners; another cross-examination pressing only the 'how' (vv.15,17).
Calvin: the wicked are zealous in baseness while believers are cool in Christ's cause; the Lord defeats them by the man's unshaken firmness.
The negative question 'will ye also become his disciples?' is the keenest irony, the 'also' implying Jesus already has disciples the Pharisees know of (John 7:45-52); the weary man turns examiner.
Wesley hears a gentler note — as if the man half-wished they too would be convinced and follow.

themes: will ye also be his disciples

schools.summary: The rare verb 'reviled' (Acts 23:4; 1 Pet 2:23) marks their angry outburst and scornful 'that fellow'; they set discipleship to Jesus against loyalty to Moses; 'Moses' disciples' a known boast.
Calvin: under the plausible pretence of fearing to revolt from the Law, they make following Christ and following Moses inconsistent — though Moses wrote of Christ.

themes: false antithesis of Moses and Christ

schools.summary: 'Whence' = His prophetic calling/authority, not birthplace; the perfect 'God hath spoken' to Moses stands on record (Ex 33:11; Heb 1:1); 'this fellow' is contemptuous; some did claim to know whence Jesus was (John 7:27).
Calvin/Wesley: they plead ignorance of His calling to avoid receiving Him as from God; Barnes: they assumed their interpretation of the law infallible.

themes: we know not whence he is

schools.summary: The man, now bold and quick, seizes the contradiction — you who know everything confess ignorance of one who opened my eyes; only divine power could do it.
Calvin: he indirectly reproves them for being unmoved by so illustrious a miracle, magnifying the wonder to expose their malice.
Wesley: the illiterate man reasons with such clearness because God opened the eyes of his understanding as well as his body.

themes: spiritual sight passes to the beggar

schools.summary: A syllogism from their own premise (v.16) and the OT (Job 27:9; Ps 66:18; Isa 1:15): God hears not sinners but worshippers; God hears this Man; therefore He is from God.
Gill/Calvin/Wesley bound the maxim — 'sinners' means the notorious/impenitent, not all who sin, for God hears every penitent; He hears only the sincere.
Clarke: 'sinners' may mean heathen/unproselytised, set against the 'worshipper'; Wesley calls the man a confessor for his honest courage (v.22).

debates.question: Who are the 'sinners' God does not hear?

sides.position: the notorious/impenitent ungodly, not all who sin

schools.summary: The cure is unprecedented and congenital — no such case in Moses or the prophets; 'since the world began' echoes Isaiah 64:4; the man rubs in the perfect 'born blind' as the decisive point.

themes: no precedent in Moses or prophets

schools.summary: A second-class condition: granting their premise that He is not from God yields 'he could do nothing' — so the man scores; one such miracle is full proof He is from God; the argument meets each of their assertions.
Clarke: God is the fountain of all good, so such beneficent miracles prove a divine commission; Wesley: nothing miraculous, of this kind.
'Altogether born in sins' is contemptuous; 'and dost thou teach us?' is insufferable audacity after he routed them; when arguments fail they revile, reverting to the view Christ rejected (v.3).
Chrysostom: those who suffer for confessing Christ are especially honoured; Lightfoot makes him the first confessor, as John the Baptist the first martyr.
Calvin: they taunt his blindness, the scribes holding that souls bear in new bodies the punishment of former crimes.

The older MSS read 'Son of Man' (Robertson, almost certainly correct) against 'Son of God'; either way a Messianic claim beyond the man's knowledge; he believes according to his light, ready to believe all Christ says.
The Catena: the temple's Lord finds the man the Jews cast out, crowning him like a victorious wrestler.
Calvin: the enemies' excommunications are trivial, for the one they cast out is sought and owned by the Son of God; Henry: Christ seeks him to comfort and reward his boldness.

debates.question: Is the reading 'Son of God' or 'Son of Man'?

sides.position: 'Son of Man' (older MSS, almost certainly correct)
'Son of God' (A L Theta, most versions)

themes: Son of God / Son of Man

schools.summary: He does not yet know it is Jesus (not having seen Him, v.7); 'Lord' means 'Sir'; he recognises the voice and catches up Christ's words, ready to believe.
Calvin: obedient and ready — 'as soon as he is pointed out, I am ready to embrace him'; Wesley: the request already implies some faith.
'Thou hast seen him' (perfect, since his eyes opened); compared to John 4:26 to the Samaritan woman, revealing Himself here as the Son of Man; a special fitness in appealing to the sight He restored.
Calvin: the disclosure carries only a small, cold portion of faith, since Christ does not yet name His death and atonement — real but seminal.
Wesley marvels at the man's deep understanding joined to a teachable temper.

themes: it is he that talketh with thee
sight as witness to the Messiah

schools.summary: The climactic short creed and act of worship; Ellicott traces the ascent from 'the man called Jesus' to prophet, to 'of God,' to Messiah adored; Robertson: 'worshipped' in John always means divine worship.
Calvin: cautiously, the homage is rare and uncommon — far more than to a prophet — true worship, whether or not his knowledge of Christ's Godhead was yet full.
'Judgment' is not condemnation (John 3:17; 12:47) but a judicial sifting/separation the Father gave the Son (John 5:22), at work in this very miracle.
Calvin: 'judgment' includes the grace of illumination — Christ restores order by a wonderful purpose contrary to human opinion.
Wesley/Clarke: the double effect — the blind in body and soul see, while the self-styled seers are given to greater blindness; sight to the Gentiles, blindness to abusers of mercy.

debates.question: What does 'for judgment I am come' mean?

sides.position: a restoring of order that includes the grace of illumination

themes: the blind see and seers blinded

schools.summary: The question expects 'no,' yet they vaguely suspect Jesus means them, recalling 'blind guides' (Mt 15:14); their proud self-confidence is the very condition that calls forth the saying.
Calvin: smitten, yet not of the worst class (they at least listen), but unprofited, since none can be Christ's disciple until divested of self.

Sin is measured by ability and opportunity; a second-class condition ('if ye were blind' — but ye are not) means real blindness would excuse, but their boast 'we see' fixes the guilt of an ignorance unwilling to be cured.
Calvin offers two readings: ignorance would lessen guilt were they not fighting the truth (John 15:22), or their ignorance might be cured would they confess it.
Wesley: invincible ignorance would excuse, but claiming sight leaves their sin without excuse or remedy.
